title: Quiz: Resource Estimation Tools
description: Test your understanding of quantum resource estimation for NISQ and fault-tolerant systems

questions.question: In fault-tolerant quantum computing with surface codes, how does the number of physical qubits scale with code distance d?
questions.explanation: For surface codes, the number of physical qubits per logical qubit scales as O(d^2), where d is the code distance. Specifically, a distance-d surface code requires approximately 2d^2 physical qubits per logical qubit.

questions.question: Why is the T-count particularly important in fault-tolerant resource estimation?

options.text: T gates take the longest to execute
T gates require magic state distillation, which consumes significant additional resources
T gates have the lowest fidelity

questions.explanation: T gates cannot be implemented transversally in most error correction codes and require magic state distillation. This process uses dedicated T-factories that consume thousands of physical qubits per factory, making T-count the dominant resource cost.

questions.question: For a NISQ circuit with estimated fidelity F, approximately how many shots are needed to achieve 5% relative error with 95% confidence?
questions.explanation: The number of shots needed scales as (1-F)/F for a given confidence level and relative error. When fidelity is low (F small), the denominator is small and (1-F) is large, requiring many more shots. As F approaches 1, fewer shots are needed.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of comparing resource estimates across different hardware platforms?

options.text: To determine which platform is cheapest
To identify which platform offers the best fidelity and feasibility for a specific circuit

questions.explanation: Platform comparison helps identify which hardware offers the best combination of fidelity, runtime, and feasibility for a specific circuit. Different platforms have different strengths (e.g., trapped ions have higher fidelity but slower gates than superconducting qubits).

questions.question: A circuit that works perfectly in simulation will have the same fidelity when executed on real quantum hardware.
questions.explanation: False. Simulators typically produce ideal results without noise or errors. Real hardware has gate errors, decoherence, readout errors, and crosstalk that significantly reduce fidelity. Resource estimation helps predict this gap.

questions.question: In Azure Quantum Resource Estimator, what does the error budget parameter control?

options.text: The maximum number of errors allowed in the circuit
The total probability of computational failure that the estimation must account for
The precision of the estimation calculation
The cost limit for cloud execution

questions.explanation: The error budget specifies the total acceptable probability of computation failure. The resource estimator then calculates the code distance and other parameters needed to achieve this target. A smaller error budget requires more resources.

questions.question: Which factor has the MOST impact on NISQ circuit fidelity?
questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates typically have error rates 10-100x higher than single-qubit gates (e.g., 99% vs 99.9% fidelity). Since fidelities multiply, the two-qubit gate count dominates the overall fidelity calculation for most circuits.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid reasons to perform resource estimation early in algorithm development? (Select all that apply)

options.text: To identify whether the algorithm is fundamentally infeasible on near-term hardware
To guide optimization efforts toward the most impactful improvements

questions.explanation: Early resource estimation helps assess feasibility (a), focus optimization efforts (b), and compare approaches (c). However, early estimates are based on assumptions and cannot provide exact predictions (d) - actual performance depends on many factors that become clear only during implementation.
